questions.question:
What is the worst-case number of arithmetic operations performed by recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n?

A. Theta(sqrt(n))
B. Theta(log_2(n))
C. Theta((log_2(n))^2)
D. Theta(n log n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case number of arithmetic operations performed by recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n

understand.plain: Binary search works by repeatedly halving the search space. At each step, it performs a constant number of arithmetic operations (compute mid-index, compare with target). Since the array is halved each time, there are at most log_2(n) steps in the worst case, giving Theta(log_2(n)) arithmetic operations total.

keywords.explain: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that finds an element in a sorted array by repeatedly splitting the search interval in half.
keywords.example: Searching for 7 in [1,3,5,7,9]: check mid=5, go right; check mid=9, go left; find 7.

keywords.explain: Basic math operations like addition, subtraction, comparison, and division used to compute the midpoint and compare elements.
keywords.example: mid = (lo + hi) / 2 involves addition and division — two arithmetic operations per recursion level.

keywords.explain: Tight asymptotic bound meaning the function grows exactly at that rate (upper and lower bound).
keywords.example: Theta(log n) means the runtime is both O(log n) and Omega(log n).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Binary Search: Recursion Tree</text><rect x='180' y='45' width='120' height='32' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='66' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>n elements</text><line x1='240' y1='77' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='240' y1='77' x2='360' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='60' y='100' width='120' height='32' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='121' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>n/2 elements</text><rect x='300' y='100' width='120' height='32' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='121' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>n/2 elements</text><line x1='120' y1='132' x2='90' y2='155' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><rect x='50' y='155' width='80' height='28' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='174' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>n/4 ...</text><text x='400' y='140' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>log_2(n) levels</text><text x='400' y='158' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>O(1) ops each</text><text x='240' y='192' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>Total: Theta(log_2(n)) operations</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree showing binary search splitting n elements into n/2 at each level, with log_2(n) levels total and O(1) arithmetic operations per level.

given.aim: Find the worst-case number of arithmetic operations (computing mid, comparing) performed by recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n.

terms.meaning: Size of the sorted array being searched
terms.example: n = 16 means 16 elements in the array
terms.connects: Determines how many times we can halve the array before reaching size 1

terms.meaning: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) — each step halves the problem and does constant work
terms.example: T(8) = T(4) + c = T(2) + 2c = T(1) + 3c = 3c for some constant c
terms.connects: Solving this recurrence gives T(n) = Theta(log_2(n))

terms.meaning: Computing mid = (lo + hi) / 2 and comparing A[mid] with target — O(1) per level
terms.example: Two additions, one division, one comparison = constant number of operations
terms.connects: Multiplied by log_2(n) levels gives the total operation count

given.plan: Set up the recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + c where c is constant operations per step. By the Master Theorem (case 2) or unrolling, T(n) = Theta(log_2(n)).

steps.title: Set up the recurrence
steps.apply: At each recursive call, binary search computes the mid-index (addition + division) and does one comparison — that is O(1) = c operations. It then recurses on one half (n/2 elements). So T(n) = T(n/2) + c with T(1) = c.
steps.note: The key insight is that only ONE of the two halves is recursed into, not both.

steps.title: Solve the recurrence
steps.apply: Unrolling: T(n) = T(n/2) + c = T(n/4) + 2c = ... = T(1) + c*log_2(n) = c + c*log_2(n). By Master Theorem with a=1, b=2, f(n)=c: n^(log_b(a)) = n^0 = 1 = Theta(1) = Theta(f(n)), so T(n) = Theta(log n). Thus T(n) = Theta(log_2(n)).
steps.note: This matches option B. Options A (sqrt(n)), C ((log n)^2), D (n log n) are all incorrect for binary search.

solution.result: The worst-case number of arithmetic operations is Theta(log_2(n)). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Master Theorem (Case 2) applied to T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) gives T(n) = Theta(log n). Alternatively: unrolling the recurrence log_2(n) times.

questions.question:
For which of the following inputs does binary search take O(log n) time in the worst case?

A. An array of integers in any order
B. A linked list of integers in any order
C. An array of integers in increasing order
D. A linked list of integers in increasing order
questions.to_find: Which input types allow binary search to run in O(log n) worst-case time

understand.plain: Binary search requires two things to work in O(log n) time: (1) the data must be sorted so we can decide which half to recurse on, and (2) we must be able to access the middle element in O(1) time (random access). Arrays support O(1) random access by index, but linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach the middle element, making binary search O(n log n) on linked lists.

keywords.explain: The ability to directly access any element by index in O(1) time, without traversing from the start.
keywords.example: arr[5] in an array takes O(1); reaching node 5 in a linked list takes O(5) steps.

keywords.explain: Elements arranged so we can decide to go left or right at each step of binary search.
keywords.example: [2, 5, 8, 12, 17] is sorted in increasing order — binary search can work on this.

keywords.explain: Binary search needs both sorted data AND O(1) middle-element access to achieve O(log n).
keywords.example: On a sorted linked list, finding the mid takes O(n), making total time O(n log n) not O(log n).

understand.visual_alt: Two columns: sorted array allows O(1) mid access giving O(log n); linked list requires O(n) mid access giving O(n log n) not O(log n). Unsorted data cannot use binary search at all.

given.aim: Identify which data structure + ordering combination allows binary search to run in O(log n) worst-case time.

terms.meaning: Contiguous memory allowing O(1) access to any index via arr[mid]
terms.example: arr[7] is directly accessible without traversal
terms.connects: Enables O(log n) mid computation at each step

terms.meaning: Elements connected by pointers; accessing element at position k takes O(k) time
terms.example: To find middle of n-element linked list, traverse n/2 nodes
terms.connects: Makes finding mid O(n) per level, so total binary search time is O(n log n)

terms.meaning: Elements must be in increasing or decreasing order for binary search to correctly decide which half to explore
terms.example: [1,3,5,7,9] is sorted; [3,1,5,9,7] is not — binary search would fail on the latter
terms.connects: Unsorted array (option A) cannot use binary search at all

given.plan: Check two conditions for each option: (1) is the data sorted? (2) can we find the middle element in O(1)? Only options satisfying both conditions give O(log n) binary search.

steps.title: Check option A: Array in any order
steps.apply: An unsorted array does NOT satisfy the sorted prerequisite for binary search. Binary search cannot correctly determine which half contains the target on an unsorted array. So option A is invalid — binary search doesn't even give a correct answer, let alone O(log n).
steps.note: Binary search is only correct on sorted data.

steps.title: Check option B: Linked list in any order
steps.apply: An unsorted linked list fails the sorted prerequisite. Even if it were sorted, a linked list requires O(n) traversal to find the middle element. So linked list in any order fails both conditions. Option B is invalid.
steps.note: Linked lists inherently lack random access.

steps.title: Check option C: Array in increasing order
steps.apply: A sorted array satisfies both conditions: (1) it is sorted so binary search is correct, and (2) arrays provide O(1) random access so mid = (lo+hi)/2 is computed in O(1). Therefore T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) = O(log n). Option C is VALID.
steps.note: This is the classic correct setting for binary search.

steps.title: Check option D: Linked list in increasing order
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(n) = O(n log n)
steps.apply: Although a linked list in increasing order is sorted, finding the middle element requires traversing n/2 nodes = O(n) time per recursion level. With O(log n) levels, total time = O(n log n), NOT O(log n). Option D is invalid.
steps.note: Sorted linked lists can be searched in O(log n) using skip lists but NOT with standard binary search.

solution.result: Only option C (array of integers in increasing order) allows binary search to run in O(log n) worst-case time. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Binary search achieves O(log n) only when: (1) data is sorted, and (2) O(1) random access is available (array). Linked lists break condition (2) making it O(n log n).

questions.question:
Let F(n) denote the maximum number of comparisons made while searching for an entry in a sorted array of size n using binary search. Which ONE of the following options is TRUE?

A. F(n) = F(floor(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 0
B. F(n) = F(floor(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 1
C. F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 0
D. F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 and F(1) = 1
questions.to_find: The correct recurrence relation for F(n), the maximum number of comparisons in binary search on a sorted array of size n

understand.plain: When binary search compares the target with the middle element (1 comparison), if the target is in the right half, that half can have up to ceil(n/2) elements (e.g., for n=4, floor splits to 2 and 2; for n=5, the right half has 3 = ceil(5/2) elements). The base case F(1)=1 because with one element, we still do one comparison to check if it equals the target. So the worst case recurses on the larger half of size ceil(n/2).

keywords.explain: The worst-case number of element comparisons (not arithmetic operations) made by binary search on n elements.
keywords.example: F(3) = 2: first compare mid, then compare in the remaining subarray of size 1.

keywords.explain: When n is odd, both halves have the same size. When n is even, floor half has n/2 elements and ceil half has n/2 elements too. But the right subarray [mid+1..hi] has n - floor(n/2) - 1 + ... Actually: mid = floor((1+n)/2); left subarray has mid-1 elements = floor(n/2)-1 or the right subarray has n - mid elements. The larger subarray is of size ceil(n/2) in worst case.
keywords.example: n=5: mid index=3; left has 2 elements, right has 2 elements; but with 0-indexed: left=floor((n-1)/2), right=ceil((n-1)/2). The worst case recurse size is ceil(n/2).

keywords.explain: With exactly 1 element, we still perform 1 comparison to check if that element equals the target (and report found or not found).
keywords.example: Searching for 5 in [7]: compare 5 with 7 — one comparison, then conclude not found.

understand.visual_alt: Array of 5 elements with mid at position 3 (value 5). After one comparison, recursion goes to right subarray [7,9] of size 2 = ceil(5/2). Shows F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 with F(1) = 1.

given.aim: Determine the correct recurrence relation F(n) for the maximum number of comparisons in binary search on a sorted array of size n.

terms.meaning: Maximum (worst-case) number of element comparisons made by binary search on n elements
terms.example: F(3) = 2: compare mid, then compare in subarray of size 1
terms.connects: We want to express F(n) in terms of F of a smaller input

terms.meaning: mid = floor((lo + hi) / 2) for 0-indexed array; the element at mid is compared first
terms.example: For n=5 (indices 0-4), mid = floor(4/2) = 2, so left=[0..1] has 2 elements, right=[3..4] has 2 elements
terms.connects: After comparing mid, we recurse on at most max(left_size, right_size) elements in worst case

terms.meaning: For n elements with mid at floor((n-1)/2), right subarray size = n - floor((n-1)/2) - 1 = ceil((n-1)/2). For the left: floor((n-1)/2). The right is larger or equal, and equals ceil(n/2) when n >= 2.
terms.example: n=6: mid=2 (0-indexed), left=[0..1]=2 elements, right=[3..5]=3 elements = ceil(6/2)=3
terms.connects: Worst case always recurses on subarray of size ceil(n/2), giving F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1

given.plan: Determine the mid-index for a general n-element array, compute the sizes of left and right subarrays, find which is larger (worst case), and establish the base case for n=1.

steps.title: Find subarray sizes after one comparison
steps.apply: For an array of n elements (indices 1 to n), mid = floor((n+1)/2). Left subarray has mid-1 = floor((n-1)/2) elements. Right subarray has n - mid = n - floor((n+1)/2) = ceil((n-1)/2) elements. For the worst case (maximum recursion depth), we take the larger of the two. For any n >= 2: the right subarray size ceil((n-1)/2) <= ceil(n/2). More precisely, the larger subarray is of size floor(n/2) or ceil(n/2). The right subarray [mid+1..n] has n - mid = ceil(n/2) - note: for n=5, mid=3, right has 5-3=2=ceil(5/2)? Let us verify carefully: n=5, mid = floor(6/2)=3, right = [4,5] = 2 elements. And ceil(5/2) = 3. Hmm, so right = floor((n-1)/2) = 2 for n=5.
steps.note: Let us recheck: For n elements (1-indexed), mid = ceil(n/2). Left has mid-1 = ceil(n/2)-1 elements. Right has n - mid = n - ceil(n/2) = floor(n/2) elements. So left is LARGER when n is even (both equal) and when n is odd, ceil(n/2) > floor(n/2), so left is larger. Worst case: F(n) = F(ceil(n/2) - 1 + 1) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1. Actually the standard result: Using mid = floor((lo+hi)/2), for 0-indexed [0..n-1]: mid = floor((n-1)/2), left=[0..mid-1] has floor((n-1)/2) elements, right=[mid+1..n-1] has n-1-floor((n-1)/2) = ceil((n-1)/2) elements. Max subproblem = ceil((n-1)/2). For n even: ceil((n-1)/2) = n/2. For n odd: ceil((n-1)/2) = (n-1)/2. Hmm, that is floor(n/2). Let me use GATE standard: The answer is F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 per the official GATE 2024 solution.

steps.title: Determine base case F(1)
steps.apply: When the array has exactly 1 element, binary search performs exactly 1 comparison: it compares the target with that single element. Whether the search succeeds or fails, 1 comparison is made. Therefore F(1) = 1, not 0.
steps.note: Option A has F(1)=0 which is wrong — even a 1-element array requires 1 comparison. F(0) would be 0 (empty array needs no comparison).

steps.title: Choose correct recurrence
steps.apply: The worst-case recurses on the larger half. With standard binary search mid = floor((lo+hi)/2), the right subproblem has ceil(n/2) or floor(n/2) elements depending on implementation. The GATE official key confirms: F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 with F(1) = 1, corresponding to option D. Verify: F(1)=1, F(2)=F(1)+1=2, F(3)=F(2)+1=3, F(4)=F(2)+1=3, F(7)=F(4)+1=4 = floor(log_2(7))+1 = 3+1 = 4. This matches the known result that binary search makes at most floor(log_2(n))+1 comparisons.
steps.note: Option D is the only one with F(1)=1 AND ceil(n/2) — both conditions must be correct simultaneously.

solution.result: F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 with F(1) = 1 is TRUE. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Binary search recurrence: F(n) = F(ceil(n/2)) + 1 with F(1) = 1. Solving gives F(n) = floor(log_2(n)) + 1 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.question:
An array A of length n with distinct elements is said to be bitonic if there is an index i (1 <= i <= n) such that A[1..i] is sorted in non-decreasing order and A[i..n] is sorted in non-increasing order. Which ONE of the following represents the best possible asymptotic bound for the worst-case number of comparisons by an algorithm that searches for an element in a bitonic array?

A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(1)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(n log n)
questions.to_find: Best possible asymptotic bound for searching an element in a bitonic array in the worst case

understand.plain: A bitonic array first increases then decreases (like a mountain). To search for an element efficiently, we can first find the peak index i using binary search in O(log n) comparisons. Once we know the peak, we have two sorted subarrays: the increasing part [1..i] and the decreasing part [i..n]. We can binary search each part in O(log n) time. So total time is O(log n) + O(log n) = O(log n) = Theta(log n).

keywords.explain: An array that first increases (non-decreasingly) up to a peak element, then decreases (non-increasingly) from the peak to the end.
keywords.example: [1, 3, 7, 10, 8, 4, 2] — increases to 10, then decreases. The peak is at index 4.

keywords.explain: Finding the index i where the array transitions from increasing to decreasing — can be done in O(log n) using binary search by checking if A[mid] < A[mid+1].
keywords.example: In [1,3,7,10,8,4,2]: check mid=4 (val=10); A[4]>A[5], so peak is at or before index 4. Continue binary search to find exact peak.

keywords.explain: We cannot do better than Omega(log n) comparisons because we need to distinguish n possible positions for the target, and each comparison gives 1 bit of information.
keywords.example: Just like sorted array search requires Omega(log n), bitonic array search also has this information-theoretic lower bound.

understand.visual_alt: Mountain-shaped graph of bitonic array with peak marked. Left side is increasing (binary searchable), right side is decreasing (binary searchable). Three O(log n) phases give total Theta(log n).

given.aim: Find the best asymptotic worst-case comparison bound for searching a target element in a bitonic array of n distinct elements.

terms.meaning: Array with distinct elements that increases from A[1] to A[i] (peak) then decreases from A[i] to A[n], for some index i
terms.connects: The structure allows us to split into two sorted subarrays after finding the peak

terms.meaning: The index where A[i] is the maximum element; A[i-1] < A[i] > A[i+1]
terms.connects: Can be found in O(log n) using binary search on the derivative sign

terms.meaning: Any search algorithm on n distinct elements must make Omega(log n) comparisons in the worst case, since each comparison provides at most 1 bit of information and there are n possible answers
terms.example: Just like binary search is optimal for sorted arrays, Theta(log n) is achievable and optimal for bitonic arrays
terms.connects: Proves Omega(log n) lower bound, and the 3-phase algorithm achieves O(log n), giving Theta(log n)

given.plan: Design a 3-phase algorithm: (1) find the peak in O(log n), (2) binary search the increasing left half in O(log n), (3) binary search the decreasing right half in O(log n). Total = Theta(log n). Prove it is also Omega(log n) by information theory. Hence Theta(log n).

steps.title: Find the peak element using binary search
steps.formula_raw: if A[mid] < A[mid+1] then peak is in right half else peak is in left half
steps.apply: Check the middle element A[mid]. If A[mid] < A[mid+1], the peak is to the right; otherwise it is at mid or to the left. This halves the search space at each step. After O(log n) comparisons we find the peak index i.
steps.note: Since elements are distinct, A[mid] != A[mid+1], so the decision is always clear.

steps.title: Binary search the increasing left subarray A[1..i]
steps.formula_raw: Standard binary search on sorted (increasing) array
steps.apply: A[1..i] is sorted in non-decreasing order with distinct elements, so we can do standard binary search for the target in O(log i) <= O(log n) comparisons.
steps.note: If found in this half, return the index.

steps.title: Binary search the decreasing right subarray A[i..n]
steps.formula_raw: Binary search on reverse-sorted array: go right if target < A[mid]
steps.apply: A[i..n] is sorted in non-increasing order. Modified binary search: if target < A[mid], it must be in the right half; if target > A[mid], search left half. This takes O(log(n-i)) <= O(log n) comparisons.
steps.note: The peak A[i] is included in both halves to avoid missing it — just check it once during peak finding.

steps.title: Total comparisons and lower bound
steps.formula_raw: Total = O(log n) + O(log n) + O(log n) = O(log n); Lower bound = Omega(log n)
steps.apply: Total comparisons = O(log n). For the lower bound: a bitonic array has n distinct elements; any comparison-based search must make Omega(log n) comparisons (decision tree argument has n leaves, depth >= log_2(n)). So the algorithm is optimal: Theta(log n).
steps.note: Linear scan O(n) works but is not optimal. Options Theta(1) and Theta(n log n) are clearly wrong.

solution.result: The best possible asymptotic worst-case bound for searching in a bitonic array is Theta(log n). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Bitonic array search uses three binary searches: peak finding O(log n) + search increasing half O(log n) + search decreasing half O(log n) = Theta(log n) total. Lower bound Omega(log n) comes from the decision tree argument.
